In fact, the best part of college was learning how to work with people and the network I built in doing that. So help. Help yourself. Go to go to office hours meet with other people. Coding, another area of challenge. Work through stuff. When you encounter a coding problem, don't go, oh, I need to get help. Someone who is wrong with my code. Look at it. What is wrong with my coat? The error messages are long. Learn how to read them. Learn how to scroll to the bottom, ignore everything and set that last line, and say, type error. Okay, what is that to OIC? I use the wrong variable. Learn how to read those messages. That's a skill we don't teach you in lecture, but probably one of the more valuable skills you'll pick up in this class. So we want you to succeed. How can you get help? I give you a few ways. Work with us. We'll work with you. People are worried. You can look online. Maybe ask questions in the ed or in and slide up. Alright, this is the fun part. are you excited about? Audio: Every year this changes. And every year I'm shocked to see this. Why is everyone so excited about Pandas? It's awful. I don't get it. It is what everyone uses. It is a skill that you get in this class that will help you in life. So I get it. You sure? Maybe you meant you were thinking it was the animal. Anyways, Pandas. Comment. Every year of events is pandas. Visualization. I like this one too. You do a lot of visualization in this class. You will teach you new tools, the professional tools and tools you would use in the workplace. You'll make cool plots. You learn how to do it. How to reason about them, how to critique other people's plots that plot is garbage used colors incorrectly. You'll learn how to do that here. Excellent. Shikko Why are people so excited about the sequel? It's ancient. It's all caps language. Like before I was born, which is way before you all were born. Sequel is very useful. People use this ancient language all the time, today, to do analysis of the latest data on the latest technology. So, good skill to have. Data? I'm excited about data too. is awesome. I'm guessing you met data 100 but we'll just go with it. This class is all about data and being excited about data, EVAs is that. and what we can do with it. Modeling will do a fair amount of modeling. Video: 1: What topic in Data 100 are you most excited about?

CDC Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) 03/25/2022.
Summary
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
What is already known about this topic?
The number of reported U.S. tuberculosis (TB) cases
decreased sharply in 2020, possibly related to multiple
factors associated with the COVID-19 pandemic.
What is incidence?
What is added by this report?
Why use it here?
Reported TB incidence (cases per 100,000 persons)
increased 9.4%, from 2.2 during 2020 to 2.4 during 2021 but
was lower than incidence during 2019 (2.7). Increases
How was "9.4%
occurred among both U.S .- born and non-U.S .- born persons.
increase" computed?
What are the implications for public health practice?
Factors contributing to changes in reported TB during 2020-
2021 likely include an actual reduction in TB incidence as
well as delayed or missed TB diagnoses. Timely evaluation
and treatment of TB and latent tuberculosis infection
remain critical to achieving U.S. TB elimination.
CDC: source, 2021

1. Using a the jupyter lab explorer tool look at the data
2. Opening the CSV directly in DataHub (read-only), Excel, Google Sheets, etc.

Let's look at the data included in the original CDC article published in 2021.
You could download the data directly from the web using Pandas:
[ ]:
tbls = pd. read_html ("https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/71/wr/mm7112al.htm?s_cid=mm7112al_w#T1_down")
df = tbls [0] # First table on the website
df
However, it is good practice to make a snapshot of the data for your analysis so we will work from a local copy.

has the convenient header parameter.

humans understand the data.

'2021': 'TB cases 2021',
'2019.1': 'TB incidence 2019',
'2020.1': 'TB incidence 2020',

52 rows x 7 columns
Wait ... but now we can't differentiate betwen the "Number of TB cases" and "TB incidence" year columns. pandas
has tried to make our lives easier by automatically adding ".1" to the latter columns, but this doesn't help us as
humans understand the data.
We can do this manually with df . rename () (documentation):
[ ]:
rename_dict = {'2019' : 'TB cases 2019' ,
'2020': 'TB cases 2020',
'2021': 'TB cases 2021',
'2019.1': 'TB incidence 2019',
'2020.1': 'TB incidence 2020',
'2021.1': 'TB incidence 2021'}
tb_df = tb_df . rename( columns=rename_dict)
tb_df

Wait ... but now we can't differentiate betwen the "Number of TB cases" and "TB incidence" year columns. pandas
has tried to make our lives easier by automatically adding ".1" to the latter columns, but this doesn't help us as
humans understand the data.
We can do this manually with df . rename () (documentation):
[ ]:
rename_dict = {'2019' : 'TB cases 2019' ,
'2020': 'TB cases 2020',
'2021': 'TB cases 2021',
'2019.1': 'TB incidence 2019',
'2020.1': 'TB incidence 2020',
'2021.1': 'TB incidence 2021'}
tb_df = tb_df . rename( columns=rename_dict)

CDC Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) 03/25/2022.
Summary
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
What is already known about this topic?
The number of reported U.S. tuberculosis (TB) cases
decreased sharply in 2020, possibly related to multiple
factors associated with the COVID-19 pandemic.
What is incidence?
What is added by this report?
Why use it here?
Reported TB incidence (cases per 100,000 persons)
increased 9.4%, from 2.2 during 2020 to 2.4 during 2021 but
was lower than incidence during 2019 (2.7). Increases
occurred among both U.S .- born and non-U.S .- born persons.
What are the implications for public health practice?
Factors contributing to changes in reported TB during 2020-
2021 likely include an actual reduction in TB incidence as
well as delayed or missed TB diagnoses. Timely evaluation
and treatment of TB and latent tuberculosis infection
remain critical to achieving U.S. TB elimination.
CDC: source, 2021

Wait ... but now we can't differentiate betwen the "Number of TB cases" and "TB incidence" year columns. pandas
has tried to make our lives easier by automatically adding ".1" to the latter columns, but this doesn't help us as
humans understand the data.
We can do this manually with df . rename () (documentation):
[ ]:
rename_dict = {'2019' : 'TB cases 2019',
'2020': 'TB cases 2020',
'2021': 'TB cases 2021',
'2019.1': 'TB incidence 2019',
'2020.1': 'TB incidence 2020',
'2021.1': 'TB incidence 2021'}
tb_df = tb_df . rename(columns=rename_dict)

Audio: So these really aren't really records in the first place We're going to do a fair amount of data cleaning in this class. You'll do it in the homework assignments. It basically boils down to using lots of PANAs functions to transform this data into a table that you'd want to work with. So I'm going to rename the unnamed columns here, it's supposed to be a geographic area. I'm going to drop the census and estimated estimates base columns. I don't need those. I want the census the global census. We do that every 10 years. I just want the actual year by year accounts. I'm going to call this magic convert d types function, which is going to, if I have an integer that's encoded as a floating point number, I'm going to make it an integer. And then we do drop in a. Drop not a number. Drop all my dance. Might be dangerous, we'll do it. Come back to dropping in a moment. Alright, so what happens when I run all these things back to back? I think Professor Noir as you said this in the last lecture, but I've done something here just as a style to make it easy to read my code. I put parentheses so that the Python interpreter knows that this is just one long line, but instead of this being one long line that goes off the page. I've put new lines in it, I've broken it down into steps. Each of these is an object, a function call on this object. This whole thing here produces a new data frame that I then apply the drop. Be well. It applies. And in order to bring these things together, we have to use The Join function. Which in pandas? is called merch. and also join, it was actually too. In this class we're going to focus on merge because it is the more general version of join. It does everything that a join should do. and it's also what you learned in the last lecture. Now I'm going to talk about how you use it and what you should be looking at in your data when you think about using it. And to do that, we need to think about ease. So when I look at my data... I need to figure out where some part of one piece of data References another part of another piece of data. And this is done by understanding the first, the concept of the primary key. Reproduce incidence
Let's recompute incidence to make sure we know where the original CDC numbers came from.
From the CDC report: TB incidence is computed as "Cases per 100,000 persons using mid-year population
estimates from the U.S. Census Bureau."
If we define a group as 100,000 people, then we can compute the TB incidence for a given state population as
TB incidence

# groups in population
population/100000

Reproduce incidence
Let's recompute incidence to make sure we know where the original CDC numbers came from.
From the CDC report: TB incidence is computed as "Cases per 100,000 persons using mid-year population
estimates from the U.S. Census Bureau."
If we define a group as 100,000 people, then we can compute the TB incidence for a given state population as
TB incidence

# TB cases in population
# groups in population
population/100000

Reproduce incidence
Let's recompute incidence to make sure we know where the original CDC numbers came from.

date.
Let's first check out this website.
Next, let's download this file, saving it as a JSON (note the source URL file type).
In the interest of reproducible data science we will download the data programatically. We have defined some
helper functions in the ds100_utils.py file. I can then reuse these helper functions in many different notebooks.

text:
Audio: So, qualitative has two subtypes. Ordinals. And nominal. Let's talk about nominal first, because that's probably the easiest of all these types. Nominal to the name. nominal is referring to names. Your name would be a nominal value. Political affiliation. Generally, it could be a nominal value. KELID number. It's actually normal. It's a number, but it's really just a way of referencing you. Don't take the average of CalID numbers that doesn't make sense. So these can be actual numbers, but you're treating them as categories as names. The one that is most tricky for students is ordinal. These are names like low, medium, and high. They haven't afforded. Preferences. Bubble of the education. Why do I need a distinction between these two things? When I look at data and I say this nominal the way I visualize it's actually very different than the data that the way I would treat ordinal data. And it's actually very different the way I would treat the data on the left here. So these quantitative fields. So when you look at data, you're going to need to figure out what kind of data it is.
